ninety seconds

SAYWHAT

two men wait for something. allegedly.

headphones · low light

browser theatre / generated variation / 90 seconds

SAYWHAT

a shapeshifting play by HrM

SayWhat is a compact absurdist play for the browser, openly inspired by Samuel Beckett’s Waiting for Godot. Two men stand at a desert crossing and wait for a message, a summons, an authority, or the moment when waiting finally admits it has no plan.

The work turns waiting into pressure. The characters talk to survive the pause, fail to repair small injuries, sulk, resume, insult, joke badly, and occasionally collapse into brawl logic. The road, sky and passing figures behave like witnesses rather than scenery.

Each run recombines dialogue, silences, nervous head-signs, bad jokes, radio voices, weather, aircraft and stage intrusions. Some intrusions belong to this world; others feel like theatre leaking through it. Pozzo and Lucky pass by ominously, as if the older play’s gravity has briefly crossed into this road.

The piece was shaped through human direction, code, browser audio and iterative AI-assisted editing. The process remains visible as rhythm, instability and variation, but the point is still theatrical: two people waiting in public until language starts to misbehave.

Runtime
90 seconds
Format
single self-contained HTML file
Mode
sound · text · moving image · generative staging
Reference
after Beckett’s Godot
Maker
HrM

Headphones help. Restarting does not repeat the same play; it restages the same pressure.

HrM / SayWhat / 90s / liminal desert
